revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]Created page with ''''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional[[Shinto]] [[Rite of passage|rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborn infants. Approximate...'
revision.text:
'''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional[[Shinto]] [[Rite of passage|rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborn infants.
Approximately one month after birth (31st day for boys and 32 days for girls), parents and grandparents bring bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have shrine priest pray for his or her health and happiness. The practice is not dissimilar a [[Christian]] [[Baptism]].
Today, most of Miyamairi is practiced between one month or 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour in turn, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought in the hall, one group after another, at a rate of about 10,000 yen per baby. Before the altar, a Shinto priest wearing unique Shinto costume and headgear appear between the group and the altar, and recite prayer or swing a [[Tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer the priest cites the name of the baby and his or her birthday. Afterwards, the parents carrying the baby go forward one by one and bow to the altar. In the end, sake, or rice wine, in a red wooden cup is given to each of them.

'''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns.
Approximately one month after birth (31st day for boys and 32 days for girls), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for his or her health and happiness. The practice is not dissimilar a [[Christian]] [[Baptism]].
Today, most of Miyamairi is practiced between one month or 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour in turn, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought in the hall, one group after another. Before the altar, a Shinto priest wearing costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[Tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents go forward one by one and bow to the altar and place tamagushi upon the alter.
In the end, sake, or rice wine, in a red wooden cup is given to each of them, or small gifts are given to the family.
A shrine will typically charge a rate of about 5,000 to 10,000 yen per baby.
{{commonscat|Shinto}}

revision.comment: removed reference to sake -- not accurate as "sake" refers to drinks other than rice wine in some parts of Japan
revision.text:
'''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns.
Approximately one month after birth (31st day for boys and 32 days for girls), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for his or her health and happiness. The practice is not dissimilar a [[Christian]] [[Baptism]].
Today, most of Miyamairi is practiced between one month or 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour in turn, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought in the hall, one group after another. Before the altar, a Shinto priest wearing costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[Tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents go forward one by one and bow to the altar and place tamagushi upon the alter.
At the end of the ceremony, rice wine in a red wooden cup is given to each attendant, or small gifts are given to the family.
A shrine will typically charge a rate of about ¥5,000 to ¥10,000 per baby.
{{commonscat|Shinto}}

'''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns.
Approximately one month after birth (31st day for boys and 32 days for girls), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for his or her health and happiness. The practice is not dissimilar a [[Christian]] [[Baptism]].
Today, most of Miyamairi is practiced between one month or 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour in turn, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought in the hall, one group after another. Before the altar, a Shinto priest wearing costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[Tamagushi|tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward, one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, rice wine in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine will typically charge between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby.
{{commonscat|Shinto}}

At the end of the ceremony, rice wine in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine will typically charge between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby.

Approximately one month after birth (31st day for boys and 32 days for girls), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for his or her health and happiness. The practice is not dissimilar a [[Christian]] [[Baptism]].
Today, most of Miyamairi is practiced between one month or 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour in turn, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought in the hall, one group after another. Before the altar, a Shinto priest wearing costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. {{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

{{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

{{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

{{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

{{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[rite of passage]] in [[Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]

{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[:en:Rite of passage|Rite of passage]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[:en:Alice Mabel Bacon|Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness. 

Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.

At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.

A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Miyamairi]][[de:Hatsumiyamairi]][[en:Miyamairi]][[id:Miyamairi]][[ja:初宮参り]][[my:မိယမအိရိ]][[pl:Miya-mairi]][[ru:Хацу-мия-маири]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q478372]] Wikidata name: Miyamairi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[:en:Rite of passage|Rite of passage]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[:en:Alice Mabel Bacon|Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]

Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Miyamairi]][[de:Hatsumiyamairi]][[en:Miyamairi]][[id:Miyamairi]][[ja:初宮参り]][[my:မိယမအိရိ]][[pl:Miya-mairi]][[ru:Хацу-мия-маири]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Miyamairi]][[de:Hatsumiyamairi]][[en:Miyamairi]][[id:Miyamairi]][[ja:初宮参り]][[my:မိယမအိရိ]][[pl:Miya-mairi]][[ru:Хацу-мия-маири]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Miyamairi]][[de:Hatsumiyamairi]][[en:Miyamairi]][[id:Miyamairi]][[ja:初宮参り]][[my:မိယမအိရိ]][[pl:Miya-mairi]][[ru:Хацу-мия-маири]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q478372]] Wikidata name: Miyamairi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] [[:en:Rite of passage|Rite of passage]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>[[:en:Alice Mabel Bacon|Alice Mabel Bacon]], "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Miyamairi]][[de:Hatsumiyamairi]][[en:Miyamairi]][[id:Miyamairi]][[ja:初宮参り]][[my:မိယမအိရိ]][[pl:Miya-mairi]][[ru:Хацу-мия-маири]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Miyamairi]][[de:Hatsumiyamairi]][[en:Miyamairi]][[id:Miyamairi]][[ja:初宮参り]][[my:မိယမအိရိ]][[pl:Miya-mairi]][[ru:Хацу-мия-маири]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Miyamairi]][[de:Hatsumiyamairi]][[en:Miyamairi]][[id:Miyamairi]][[ja:初宮参り]][[my:မိယမအိရိ]][[pl:Miya-mairi]][[ru:Хацу-мия-маири]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q478372]] Wikidata name: Miyamairi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto rite of passage}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2011}} [[File:Okunitama_2008-3-29_Miyamairi_girl-2.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|Attending a ''miyamairi'' at a shrine in Tokyo]] '''Miyamairi''' (宮参り, literally "shrine visit") is a traditional [[Shinto]] {{ill|Rite of passage|en|Rite of passage|WD=Q1131696|lt=Rite of passage}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} for newborns. Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>{{ill|Alice Mabel Bacon|en|Alice Mabel Bacon|WD=Q2836611|lt=Alice Mabel Bacon}}, "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]

Approximately one month after birth (31 days for boys and 33 days for girls<ref>{{ill|Alice Mabel Bacon|en|Alice Mabel Bacon|WD=Q2836611|lt=Alice Mabel Bacon}}, "Japanese Girls and women" revised and enlarged edition, Boston and New York, Houghton Mifflin Company, The Riverside Press Cambridge, 1902</ref>), parents and grandparents bring the child to a Shinto shrine, to express gratitude to the deities for the birth of a baby and have a shrine priest pray for their health and happiness.
Today, most Miyamairi are practiced between one month and 100 days after birth. In famous and busy shrines, the ceremony is held every hour, often during weekends. A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.

A group of a dozen babies and their families are usually brought into the hall, one group after another. A Shinto priest wearing a costume and headgear appears between the group and the altar, reciting a prayer and swinging a [[tamagushi]] right and left. During the prayer, the priest cites the name of the baby, the names of the parents, the family's address, and the baby's birthday. Afterwards, the parents and grandparents come forward one by one, bow to the altar, and place tamagushis upon it.
At the end of the ceremony, sake in a red wooden cup is given to each person in attendance; small gifts are often given to the family.
A shrine typically charges between ¥5,000 and ¥10,000 per baby for this ceremony.
